How Retrieval Practice Strengthens Reading Memory

Retrieval practice strengthens reading memory because the learner must rebuild the idea from cues instead of merely recognizing familiar sentences. The act of reconstructing the claim exposes missing links, makes attention measurable, and gives the next reread a concrete job.

A useful companion waits until the reader has had time to inspect the paragraph, then asks for the causal mechanism in the reader's own words. A weak answer usually repeats the topic while missing the mechanism: rebuilding from cues reveals gaps and guides the next pass.

Why waiting matters

The policy should prefer a quiet checkpoint over constant interruption. If the companion jumps in before the reader has oriented to the section, the prompt feels like noise rather than help.

Once the reader slows down, revisits a sentence, or lingers near a dense explanation, the same prompt can feel well timed. The difference is not only whether the model can ask a question; it is whether the runtime state machine has enough evidence that the question belongs.

What a good interruption checks

The best interruption asks the reader to connect a cause with an effect. In this passage, the important cause is reconstructing an idea from memory cues, and the important effect is exposing gaps that guide a more focused reread.

A vague answer such as "I am not sure" should not be celebrated. The companion should treat it as a signal to clarify the expected point, offer feedback, and avoid pretending the reader mastered the concept.
